title: Rates of Change in Science and Engineering

epigraph.text: He makes grass grow for the cattle, and plants for people to cultivate—bringing forth food from the earth.
epigraph.quote: The book of nature is written in the language of mathematics.

content.text:
You have spent six sections learning *how* to differentiate. You can 
handle polynomials, exponentials, trigonometric functions, logarithms, 
compositions, and implicit curves. Now we ask the deeper question: 
**what does the derivative *mean**?*

The answer, of course, is *rate of change*. That was the definition 
all along. But the phrase "rate of change" takes on strikingly different 
meanings depending on whether you are a physicist tracking a falling stone, 
a biologist culturing bacteria, an economist analyzing production costs, or 
an engineer designing a circuit. In every case, the mathematics is the 
same—the derivative—but the interpretation changes with the context.

This section is a guided tour through several scientific disciplines. 
Our goal is twofold: to see the derivative at work in the real world, and 
to practice the differentiation rules of this chapter on functions that 
arise naturally in science and engineering.
Physics: Velocity and Acceleration

Suppose an object moves along a straight line (say, a ball tossed straight 
up or a car traveling down a highway). Let $s(t)$ denote the 
**position** of the object at time $t$.

content.title: Velocity and Acceleration
content.content:
If $s(t)$ gives the position of an object at time $t$, then:

1. The **velocity** is $v(t) = s'(t) = \deriv{s}{t}$.

2. The **acceleration** is 
 $a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t) = \dderiv{s}{t}$.

3. The **speed** is $\abs{v(t)}$.

content.text:
Velocity tells us the *rate* at which position changes, and its sign 
tells us the *direction*: positive velocity means the object is moving 
in the positive direction; negative velocity means it is moving in the 
negative direction. Acceleration tells us the rate at which *velocity* 
changes.

content.title: Free-fall under gravity
content.problem:
A ball is dropped from the top of a 200-foot building. Ignoring air 
resistance, the height of the ball above the ground at time $t$ seconds is
\[
 s(t) = 200 - 16t^{2}.
\]
Find: (a) the velocity after 2 seconds, (b) the acceleration, and 
(c) the time at which the ball hits the ground and its speed at that moment.
content.solution:
**(a)** We differentiate using the Power Rule and the Constant Rule:
\[
 v(t) = s'(t) = \deriv{}{t}\bigl[200 - 16t^{2}\bigr] = -32t.
\]
At $t = 2$: $v(2) = -32(2) = -64$ ft/s. The negative sign tells us the 
ball is falling (moving in the negative direction, i.e., downward).

**(b)** Differentiating again:
\[
 a(t) = v'(t) = \deriv{}{t}[-32t] = -32 \text{ ft/s}^{2}.
\]
The acceleration is constant—this is Earth's gravitational acceleration 
(approximately $-32$ ft/s$^{2}$, or $-9.8$ m/s$^{2}$ in metric units).

**(c)** The ball hits the ground when $s(t) = 0$:
\[
 200 - 16t^{2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t^{2} = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = \sqrt{12.5} = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} \approx 3.54 \text{ s}.
\]
The velocity at impact is
\[
 v\!\left(\tfrac{5\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = -32 \cdot \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{2} 
 = -80\sqrt{2} \approx -113.1 \text{ ft/s}.
\]
The *speed* at impact is $\abs{v} = 80\sqrt{2} \approx 113.1$ ft/s.

content.title: Projectile motion
content.problem:
A model rocket is launched vertically with an initial velocity of 
$128$ ft/s from ground level. Its height at time $t$ is given by
\[
 s(t) = 128t - 16t^{2}.
\]
Find (a) the maximum height and (b) the total time of flight.
content.solution:
**(a)** The rocket reaches its maximum height when $v(t) = 0$:
\[
 v(t) = s'(t) = 128 - 32t = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 4 \text{ s}.
\]
The maximum height is $s(4) = 128(4) - 16(16) = 512 - 256 = 256$ ft.

We can verify this is a maximum (not a minimum) by checking that 
$a(t) = s''(t) = -32 < 0$: the acceleration is negative, so the velocity 
is decreasing. At $t = 4$ the velocity passes through zero from positive 
to negative, confirming a peak.

**(b)** The rocket returns to the ground when $s(t) = 0$:
\[
 128t - 16t^{2} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 16t(8 - t) = 0 
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t = 0 \text{ or } t = 8.
\]
The total time of flight is $8$ seconds. Notice the symmetry: the rocket 
takes $4$ seconds to go up and $4$ seconds to come back down.

content.text: Biology: Population Growth

In biology, one of the most fundamental quantities is a **population** 
$P(t)$ as a function of time. The derivative $\deriv{P}{t}$ measures the 
**growth rate**—how fast the population is increasing (or decreasing) 
at a given instant.

The simplest model of population growth is **exponential growth**, 
in which the rate of growth is proportional to the current population:
\[
 \deriv{P}{t} = kP,
\]
where $k > 0$ is a constant called the **growth rate constant**. 
The solution to this equation (which we will derive rigorously in a later 
chapter) is
\[
 P(t) = P_{0} e^{kt},
\]
where $P_{0} = P(0)$ is the initial population.

Let us verify this: if $P(t) = P_{0} e^{kt}$, then by the Chain Rule,
\[
 \deriv{P}{t} = P_{0} \cdot k e^{kt} = k \cdot P_{0} e^{kt} = kP(t). \quad\checkmark
\]

content.title: Bacterial growth
content.problem:
A bacterial colony starts with $500$ cells and doubles every $3$ hours. 
Find (a) the growth rate constant $k$, (b) the population after $10$ hours, 
and (c) the rate of growth after $10$ hours.
content.solution:
**(a)** We have $P_{0} = 500$ and $P(3) = 1000$. Setting up:
\[
 1000 = 500 e^{3k} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2 = e^{3k} 
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3k = \ln 2 
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k = \frac{\ln 2}{3} \approx 0.2310.
\]

**(b)** After $10$ hours:
\[
 P(10) = 500 e^{10 \cdot (\ln 2)/3} = 500 e^{(10\ln 2)/3} 
 = 500 \cdot 2^{10/3} \approx 500 \cdot 10.079 \approx 5040 \text{ cells}.
\]

**(c)** The growth rate is
\[
 \deriv{P}{t}\bigg|_{t=10} = kP(10) = \frac{\ln 2}{3} \cdot 5040 
 \approx 0.2310 \cdot 5040 \approx 1164 \text{ cells/hour}.
\]
The colony is adding roughly $1164$ new cells per hour at the $10$-hour 
mark.

content.title: Exponential growth cannot last forever
content.content:
The model $P(t) = P_{0}e^{kt}$ predicts unbounded growth. In reality, 
populations encounter limited resources, disease, and competition. More 
realistic models (like the **logistic model**, which you will meet 
in differential equations) account for a carrying capacity. The 
exponential model is excellent for *early-stage* growth when 
resources are plentiful.

content.text: Economics: Marginal Analysis

Economists use derivatives constantly, though they call them by different 
names. If $C(x)$ is the total cost of producing $x$ units of a good, 
then the derivative $C'(x)$ is called the **marginal cost**.

content.title: Marginal Cost, Revenue, and Profit
content.content:
Let $x$ denote the number of units produced (or sold).

1. If $C(x)$ is the **total cost** function, then 
 $C'(x)$ is the **marginal cost**—the approximate cost of 
 producing one additional unit.

2. If $R(x)$ is the **total revenue** function, then 
 $R'(x)$ is the **marginal revenue**—the approximate 
 additional revenue from selling one more unit.

3. The **profit** function is $P(x) = R(x) - C(x)$, and 
 the **marginal profit** is $P'(x) = R'(x) - C'(x)$.

content.text:
Why does $C'(x)$ approximate the cost of one more unit? Because
\[
 C'(x) \approx \frac{C(x+1) - C(x)}{1} = C(x+1) - C(x).
\]
The derivative is the limit of this difference quotient as the 
increment approaches zero, so for integer values of $x$, $C'(x)$ is a 
close approximation to the actual cost of the $(x+1)$-st unit.

content.title: Marginal cost and revenue
content.problem:
A small company manufactures artisanal candles. The cost (in dollars) of 
producing $x$ candles per week is
\[
 C(x) = 0.001x^{3} - 0.15x^{2} + 12x + 800,
\]
and the revenue from selling $x$ candles at price $p(x) = 25 - 0.02x$ 
dollars each is
\[
 R(x) = x \cdot p(x) = 25x - 0.02x^{2}.
\]
Find (a) the marginal cost and marginal revenue when $x = 100$, and 
(b) the production level at which profit is maximized (approximately).
content.solution:
**(a)** Differentiating:
$$
 C'(x) &= 0.003x^{2} - 0.30x + 12, \\
 R'(x) &= 25 - 0.04x.
$$
At $x = 100$:
$$
 C'(100) &= 0.003(10000) - 0.30(100) + 12 = 30 - 30 + 12 = \__MATH_BLOCK_4__21.
$$
The $101$st candle costs approximately $\$12$ to produce but brings in 
approximately $\$21$ in revenue. Since $R'(100) > C'(100)$, the company 
should increase production.

**(b)** Profit is maximized (approximately) where marginal revenue 
equals marginal cost:
\[
 R'(x) = C'(x) \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 
 25 - 0.04x = 0.003x^{2} - 0.30x + 12.
\]
Rearranging:
\[
 0.003x^{2} - 0.26x - 13 = 0.
\]
By the quadratic formula:
\[
 x = \frac{0.26 \pm \sqrt{0.0676 + 0.156}}{0.006} 
 = \frac{0.26 \pm \sqrt{0.2236}}{0.006} 
 = \frac{0.26 \pm 0.4729}{0.006}.
\]
Taking the positive root: $x \approx \dfrac{0.7329}{0.006} \approx 122$ 
candles. (We will develop rigorous optimization techniques in 
Chapter [ch:applications].)

content.text: Chemistry: Reaction Rates

In chemistry, a **reaction rate** measures how fast the concentration 
of a reactant decreases (or a product increases) over time. If $[A](t)$ 
denotes the concentration of substance $A$ at time $t$ (usually in 
mol/L), then the rate of reaction is
\[
 \text{rate} = -\deriv{[A]}{t}.
\]
The negative sign is a convention: since the reactant is being consumed, 
$\deriv{[A]}{t} < 0$, and we want the rate to be positive.

content.title: First-order reaction
content.problem:
In a first-order decomposition, the concentration of a reactant decays 
exponentially:
\[
 [A](t) = [A]_{0}\, e^{-kt},
\]
where $[A]_{0}$ is the initial concentration and $k > 0$ is the rate 
constant. Suppose the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide 
(H$_{2}$O$_{2}$) in a solution has $[A]_{0} = 0.80$ mol/L and 
$k = 0.035$ min$^{-1}$. Find the reaction rate at $t = 20$ minutes.
content.solution:
Using the Chain Rule:
\[
 \deriv{[A]}{t} = [A]_{0} \cdot (-k) e^{-kt} = -k \cdot [A]_{0}\, e^{-kt}.
\]
The reaction rate is therefore
\[
 \text{rate} = -\deriv{[A]}{t} = k \cdot [A]_{0}\, e^{-kt}.
\]
At $t = 20$:
\[
 \text{rate} = 0.035 \cdot 0.80 \cdot e^{-0.035(20)} 
 = 0.028 \cdot e^{-0.70} \approx 0.028 \cdot 0.4966 
 \approx 0.0139 \text{ mol/(L$\cdot$min)}.
\]
The concentration is decreasing at about $0.014$ mol/L per minute at the 
$20$-minute mark.

content.text: Electrical Engineering: Current and Charge

In an electrical circuit, **current** is the rate at which charge 
flows past a point. If $Q(t)$ denotes the charge (in coulombs) that has 
flowed past a point at time $t$ (in seconds), then the current is
\[
 I(t) = \deriv{Q}{t}.
\]
Current is measured in **amperes** (A), where $1$ A $= 1$ C/s.

content.title: Current from a charge function
content.problem:
The charge flowing through a component is given by
\[
 Q(t) = 5\sin(120\pi t) + 3t \quad\text{(coulombs)},
\]
where $t$ is in seconds. Find the current $I(t)$ and evaluate $I(1/240)$.
content.solution:
Differentiating using the Chain Rule and the Power Rule:
\[
 I(t) = \deriv{Q}{t} = 5 \cdot \cos(120\pi t) \cdot 120\pi + 3 
 = 600\pi\cos(120\pi t) + 3.
\]
At $t = 1/240$:
\[
 I\!\left(\tfrac{1}{240}\right) = 600\pi\cos\!\left(\frac{120\pi}{240}\right) + 3 
 = 600\pi\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) + 3 = 600\pi(0) + 3 = 3 \text{ A}.
\]
At this instant the sinusoidal component of the current is zero, and only 
the steady $3$ A contribution remains.

content.text: A Unifying Perspective

Let us pause and notice something remarkable. In each discipline above, 
we performed the same mathematical operation—differentiation—but 
the interpretation changed entirely:

This universality is one of the deepest features of calculus. The same 
mathematical structure governs falling apples, growing colonies, business 
decisions, chemical reactions, and electrical circuits. It is as though 
the Creator wove a single thread of logic through all of nature.

marginNotes.text:
There is a time for everything, and a 
season for every activity under the heavens.

God blessed them and said, "Be fruitful 
and increase in number and fill the water in the seas, and let the birds 
increase on the earth."

When he established the force of the wind 
and measured out the waters, when he made a decree for the rain and a 
path for the thunderstorm—then he looked at wisdom and appraised it.

For since the creation of the world God's 
invisible qualities—his eternal power and divine nature—have been 
clearly seen, being understood from what has been made.

marginNotes.author: Galileo Galilei
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the language in which God has 
written the universe.

marginNotes.author: Alfred Marshall
marginNotes.text:
Economics is the study of mankind in the 
ordinary business of life.

marginNotes.author: Michael Faraday
marginNotes.text:
Nothing is too wonderful to be true, if it 
be consistent with the laws of nature.
